metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_4451
metadata.comment:
Rationale: This annotation is part of a systematic annotation of all synaptic vesicle V-ATPases.

Rationale: The luminal V-ATPase is known to be present in the membrane of synaptic vesicles where it drives the import of neurotransmitter molecules via a range of antiporter protein complexes (PMID:28383767). The mammalian V-ATPases consists of a peripheral V1 domain and an integral V0 domain that together play a role in synaptic vesicle proton loading (PMID: 16524922, 32165585). The V1 domain is known to be involved in ATP hydrolysis while the V0 domain is known to be involved in proton translocation across the synaptic vesicle membrane. The annotated paper explores the role of V-ATPase in synaptic vesicle proton loading with regards to synaptic vesicle endo/exocytosis.

Figure 1B: V-ATPase tagged subunits expressed in hippocampal neurons shows a punctate fluorescent pattern and colocalises with presynaptic marker Synaptobrevin2-pHluorin (Syb2-pHl) indicating a localisation in presynaptic boutons.

Figure 4A and S4: Fluorescence recovery curves of GFP-V1B2 show a decrease in ampltidue in silence conditions (0 mM Ca2+, 1 mM TTX) compared to a depolarised condition (45 mM K+). Similar profiles were observed for subunits GFP-V1A and GFP-V1H. Upon lumenal pH neutralisation (NH4Cl) and alkalinization (FCCP) conditions, fluorescence recovery of GFP-V1B2 is almost completely abolished indicating a crucial role of intravesicular pH as a regulator of V-ATPase assembly at presynaptic boutons.

Figure 5A-C: A significant decrease in cumulative exocytosis amplitude is observed in the presence of SaliPhe (70% for V0c-pHl and 65% for Syb2-pHl) indicating that pharmocologically locking the assembly of V-ATPase leads to a decline in release.

Figure 7E: Schematic depicts the role of V-ATPase assembly/disassembly in NT uptake coupled to the SV exo/endocytosis cycle.". Experimental description: Dissociated hippocampal cultures from postnatal day 0
